does: This paper provides a semantic and historical analysis of the term 'mechanistic interpretability,' identifying and distinguishing four distinct uses: a narrow technical definition requiring causal claims about model behavior, a broader technical definition encompassing any investigation of model internals, a narrow cultural definition describing a specific research community and movement, and a broad cultural definition that now encompasses interpretability research more generally. The authors trace how the term's meaning has drifted and proliferated through detailed historical examination of the NLP interpretability and mechanistic interpretability communities, documenting their divergence and subsequent convergence around shared terminology despite different research traditions and standards.
connects: This work engages with the extensive literature on neural network interpretability that emerged from work on attention mechanisms, probing tasks, and feature visualization, while acknowledging the parallel development of mechanistic interpretability as a distinct community focused on circuit analysis and causal claims about neural computation. It responds to foundational debates about what constitutes 'understanding' neural models—whether through behavioral analysis, causal intervention, or systematic decomposition of learned representations—drawing on decades of work in explainability and interpretability across machine learning. The paper also connects to recent discussions in philosophy of science and conceptual analysis about how technical terminology gets adopted, modified, and reinterpreted within scientific communities.
enables: This clarification work sets a foundation for more rigorous communication and collaboration within interpretability research by making explicit the different meanings researchers invoke when discussing 'mechanistic' work. By documenting the historical and cultural dimensions of this polysemy, the paper enables future research to either adopt more precise terminology or explicitly negotiate what interpretation is intended. This could facilitate better citation practices, more targeted literature reviews, and clearer assessment of claims in mechanistic interpretability—distinguishing between causal discoveries versus descriptive characterizations of model internals. It also opens space for productive dialogue between communities with different epistemological standards, potentially unifying interpretability research around shared goals despite methodological differences.
